texts.full_text: ross different age groups. For example, the younger generation of female internal migrants has levels of mental distress than their older counterparts ([@bb0150]). The above studies suggest a need for further research on the effects of migration and life transitions in general on students' social adaptation and adjustment to college life (see also [@bb0165]). The present study focuses on this issue. It is conducted among Chinese international students who are studying at universities in Germany, as well as their non-chinese german counterparts. the sections provide a brief overview of the research context---Germany---and its most impo

texts.preview_tail: This method enables you to retrieve all the enabled components and their nested enabled component screens in a single database, instead of having to perform three distinct requests as you mentioned in
texts.full_text: that by projecting what you have into an anonymous type: var query=context.Modules .Where(m=>m.IsEnabled && m.ModuleScreen.IsEnabled) .Select(m=>new { Id = m.Id, IsEnabled = m.IsEnabled, ModuleScreen = m.ModuleScreen.Where(ms=>ms.IsEnabled) }); This will get you a single query that retrieves all enabled components and their nested enabled module screens. Note that I'm saying you're using Entity Framework as your ORM, and that you have a `context` object that reflects your DB context. However, I'm creating the `Where` method to filter the results based on the `IsEnabled` property of the `Module` and `ModuleScreen` entities, and the `Select` method to project the results into an anonymous type with the desired properties. This method enables you to retrieve all the enabled components and their nested enabled component screens in a single database, instead of having to perform three distinct requests as you mentioned in
